feedingProfile.preferredNpk: balanced N-rich (apply ~50-60% of annual nitrogen in Mar-Apr, balance May-Jul)

commonIssues: Dioecious: a separate male vine is required to pollinate female vines for fruit; one male can pollinate several females. Vines cannot be sexed until they bloom.
Spring shoots are very frost tender; spring frost below about -1 C (30 F) damages new growth, and an early fall frost can damage unharvested fruit.
Requires 600-700 winter chilling hours below 7 C (45 F) and roughly 225-240 frost-free days to crop, so it fruits reliably only in USDA zones 7-9.
Vigorous, heavy vine that needs a permanent, strong trellis, T-bar, arbor, or pergola; never let it climb a tree or weak structure.

Container note: this is naturally a large in-ground vine (15-30 ft). The recommended minimum container is a conservative large pot (~76 L / 20 US gal); fruiting in a container is reduced versus in-ground and still demands a sturdy trellis.
High water demand; provide good drainage to avoid root rot, especially in containers where overwatering risk is higher.
Does not bear a first crop until roughly the 3rd-4th year after planting.

source.citation: Oregon State University Extension Service, EM 9322 'Growing kiwifruit in your home garden': cold hardy to about 0-10 F; 225-240 frost-free days; full sun preferred; fertile well-drained sandy/clay loam, pH 5.6-6.5; 0.5-1 lb N per vine/year (two-thirds in March, one-third May/June); permanent strong T-bar (6-7 ft) or arbor (7-8 ft) trellis required; one male per female; first crop in the third year.
Oregon State University Extension Service, EM 9322: requires a permanent strong trellis (T-bar or arbor); one male vine pollinates female vines; vines are dioecious and only sexable at bloom.
Oregon State University Extension Service, EM 9322: fertilize with 0.5-1 lb nitrogen per vine per year, applying two-thirds in March and one-third in May/June.

North Carolina State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox, Actinidia chinensis var. deliciosa: USDA zones 7a-9b; full sun (6+ hours direct sunlight a day) or dappled shade with reduced fruiting; needs good drainage / well-drained soil; deciduous twining/climbing woody vine 15-30 ft tall, 6-10 ft wide; sturdy trellis recommended; dioecious, needs both a male and a female to fruit; spring flowering, fruit ripens in early fall.

North Carolina State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox: deciduous twining climbing/spreading woody vine reaching 15-30 ft; a sturdy trellis is recommended; can be trained on an arbor, patio overhead, wall, or fence.

UC ANR Small Farms Network, Kiwifruit Production in California: bearing plants irrigated three to four times per week (young vines daily or every two days for the first 3 years); requires 600-700 winter chilling hours below 45 F and 225-240 frost-free days; minimum safe winter temperature about 10-15 F; spring frost damage below 30 F; ~1 lb nitrogen per vine for full-bearing vines with 50-60% applied Mar-Apr and balance May-Jul; soil pH below 7.3; ~4 years to a commercial crop.

UC ANR Small Farms Network: full-bearing vines take about 1 lb nitrogen per plant; apply at least 50-60% in March-April and the balance in May, June and July; young vines scale by age. Used to set feeding interval and N timing.

UC ANR Small Farms Network: winter chilling requirement 600-700 hours below 45 F, minimum safe winter temperature about 10-15 F (~-12 to -9 C), spring frost damage below 30 F; basis for coldHardyMinC and frostSensitive.

Confidence note: baseIntervalDays=2 is derived from extension guidance to irrigate bearing vines 'three to four times per week' (OSU/UC ANR) and conservatively reflects this plant's high water demand; exact day-interval is climate dependent. hotWeatherMultiplier (0.5) and rainSkipThresholdMm (12) are reasonable estimates not given as explicit numbers in the sources.

Confidence note: heatStressC=35 is an estimate. Reviewed extension pages emphasize cold/frost tolerance and chilling requirement but do not state an explicit upper heat-stress temperature for Actinidia deliciosa; 35 C is a conservative placeholder pending an authoritative figure.

Confidence note: recommendedMinLiters=76 (~20 US gal) and idealMinLiters=95 are converted from horticulture guidance that fuzzy kiwi needs a final container of at least 20 gallons (larger for vigorous fuzzy types). This is a container-adaptation estimate; A. deliciosa is naturally a large in-ground vine and the university extension sources treat it as ground/trellis grown rather than a container crop.
